1:
The chapter opens on the brief, disappointing reign of Rehoboam's son, and the witnesses fasten first on his very name. Matthew Henry hears a rebuke in the spelling: in 2 Chron. 13, where his war and God-given victory over Jeroboam are told and no wickedness is charged, he is Abijah — 'my father is the LORD'; but here, where his faults are named, the divine syllable Jah is stripped away in disgrace and he is left plainly Abijam. Charles Ellicott, working the grammatical-historical angle, is more guarded: the fuller form 'Abijah' is probably correct as the more significant, and the variation here, if not a mere false reading, may have been made to distinguish this king from Jeroboam's son of the same name (1 Kings 14:1).

On the bare chronology the voices agree. John Gill observes that Abijam began to reign in Jeroboam's eighteenth year, which proves that Rehoboam died after a seventeen-year reign; Henry adds that this son reigned scarcely three years, cut off — made proud and secure by his great victory (2 Chron. 13:21) — to make way for the better man, Asa. Adam Clarke simply frames the section, referring the reader for Abijam, his brethren, and Rehoboam's family to the fuller record in 2 Chronicles 12.

The genealogy draws comment too. Henry identifies the mother Maachah as a descendant of Absalom, David's son, reasoning that Rehoboam pointedly married within the royal house — one wife a daughter of Jerimoth, another of David's brother Eliab — taking warning by his father not to marry strangers, yet thinking it below him to marry commoners.

The chapter's verdict, however, is moral, and here Henry and Gill speak as one. Abijam was not like David: he had no hearty affection for God's ordinances, though he could plead his possession of temple and priesthood when it served his quarrel with Jeroboam (2 Chron. 13:10). 'Many boast of their profession of godliness,' Henry warns, 'who are strangers to the power of it.' His heart was not perfect with the LORD his God; he wanted sincerity, began fairly well, and fell off, walking in all his father's sins though he had seen their bad consequences. That he was continued on the throne at all was, both agree, purely for David's sake — that David might have the promised lamp (Ps. 132:17) and Jerusalem be established — which only aggravates the sin of a degenerate seed who fare the better for a piety they refuse to imitate.
2:
The notice of the reign's length and of the king's mother is short, but the witnesses draw two exact points from it. On the three years, John Gill insists the figure is rounded: 'three only, and indeed not three full years, only one whole year and part of two others,' since Asa began to reign in Jeroboam's twentieth year — so Abijam held part of the eighteenth, the whole nineteenth, and part of the twentieth. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse together with v.3: Abijam walked as king in his father's footsteps, and although he made presents to the temple (v.15), his heart was not shalem, not wholly and undividedly given to the LORD as David's was (compare 1 Kings 11:4).

The larger interest gathers on the mother, Maachah the daughter of Abishalom. The grammatical-historical voices identify Abishalom with Absalom, David's rebel son, whose own mother (2 Sam. 3:3) was likewise named Maachah. Ellicott presses the difficulty of the parallel record: in the Chronicles she is called Michaiah the daughter of Uriel of Gibeah (2 Chron. 13:2), which shows — as chronological considerations would in any case suggest — that she must have been not the daughter but the granddaughter of Absalom, Uriel having married Absalom's daughter Tamar. Scripture's loose use of 'daughter' for a descendant thus dissolves the apparent clash between the two books.

Ellicott adds the biographical colour that the coming verses will need. Of all Rehoboam's wives — 'eighteen wives and threescore concubines' — Maachah was the favourite, so that even in his lifetime Rehoboam exalted Abijam to be ruler among his brethren (2 Chron. 11:21). She is the same woman who reappears below (v.13) as prominent in the very idolatry Asa will have to purge from the court. The witnesses do not quarrel here; they agree that the short reign, the doubtful heart, and the idolatrous queen-mother together set the stage — the house of David preserved, but preserved in a line that has already begun to decline, and held up only by the promise made to its founder.
3:
The formula of condemnation — he walked in all the sins of his father, and his heart was not perfect with the LORD his God — is read by the witnesses as a study in half-hearted religion. John Gill takes the 'sins of his father' to mean chiefly idolatrous practices, the son copying both parents. Adam Clarke makes the imperfect heart plainer still: 'he was an idolater, or did not support the worship of the true God,' and takes this to be the settled sense of the phrase throughout these histories — a heart not perfect with God is a heart that will not give him undivided worship.

Charles Ellicott presses the irony hardest. The adoption of Rehoboam's idolatries did not, he notes, prevent Abijam from posing as the champion of the temple and priesthood against Jeroboam's rival worship (2 Chron. 13:4–12), and even from dedicating treasures — perhaps the spoils of his victory — in the house of the LORD. From the qualified phrase 'his heart was not perfect before God' Ellicott infers that, like Solomon and Rehoboam before him, Abijam professed to worship Jehovah only as the supreme God of a pantheon. Hence 'a curious irony of circumstance,' that he is recorded inveighing against the degradation of God's worship in Israel while he himself countenanced or connived at the worse sin of rival gods in Judah.

The three voices converge on a single pastoral point, and it is the great theme of the whole chapter's contrast between kings. Sincerity, not ceremonial zeal, is the test God applies. Abijam had temple, priesthood, victory, and a plausible cause; what he lacked was the undivided heart, and the lack undid all the rest. The measure set beside him is fixed and unyielding — as the heart of David his father — so that even a king who can plead orthodoxy against his neighbours is weighed and found wanting when his own devotion is mixed. The reformation his son Asa will undertake is already implied here in what Abijam left undone, and the reader is being taught to look past a man's boasts to the state of his heart before the LORD.
4:
Against the dark verdict on Abijam the text sets a bright nevertheless, and the witnesses gather on the image of the lamp. John Gill reads it, with the Targum, as a kingdom in Jerusalem — 'splendid and glorious' — to be continued in David's posterity. Adam Clarke narrows the figure concretely: the lamp is 'a son to succeed him,' the same promise given in 1 Kings 11:36. The difference is one of emphasis, not substance; all take the lamp as the unbroken survival of David's line when it deserved, on its own account, to be snuffed out.

Keil and Delitzsch hold the two clauses together — to set up his son after him, and to establish Jerusalem — and stress the little word ki, 'because': it was only for David's sake, and because David had done right, that Jehovah did not reject Abijam but let the throne pass to his son. Charles Ellicott supplies the historical hinge the verse assumes: behind it lies the great victory recorded in the Chronicles (2 Chron. 13:3–20), which was the turning-point that saved the lamp of David's house from extinction and established Jerusalem in security.

Ellicott then draws the phrase 'for David's sake' to its root — the fulfilment of the covenant promise sworn to David (2 Sam. 7:12–16) — and turns it into a meditation on the moral order of history. 'In virtue of the continuity of human history, the Divine law always ordains that, in respect of consequences, the good deeds as well as the sins of fathers are visited on their children.' The same principle that the previous verses used against Abijam — that a degenerate son inherits the effects of his fathers' evil — now works for him: he is upheld not for his own worth but for the long faithfulness of an ancestor three generations back. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that grace here runs along the channel of a promise, and that the survival of the throne is a monument less to Abijam than to the God who keeps his word to David.
5:
The ground of David's standing, and the one blot on his record, occupy the witnesses here. On the praise, John Gill reads David did that which was right in the eyes of the LORD as covering both worship and walk: he turned not aside from anything God commanded, 'especially in matters of religion, nor even in his moral walk and conversation, deliberately, studiously, and with design' — the qualification guarding against the charge that David was sinless.

The exception, save only in the matter of Uriah the Hittite, is handled with striking candour. Adam Clarke calls it 'properly speaking, the only flagrant fault or crime in the life of David,' yet 'a horrible offense, or rather a whole system of offenses,' pointing the reader to 2 Samuel 11 and 2 Samuel 12 for the murder and the adultery together. Matthew Henry, at the chapter's head, had drawn the same balance: it was a remaining blot on David's name, the reproach of which was not wiped away though the guilt was, and yet, being repented of, it did not throw him out of the covenant nor cut off the entail of the promise upon his seed.

Charles Ellicott raises the text-critical question the phrase invites. In this passage alone do we find this qualification of the praise of David, and in the Vatican manuscript and other copies of the Septuagint the clause is omitted; possibly, he suggests, it is a marginal note that crept into the text, or a comment of the book's compiler upon the annals from which he drew. The witnesses do not finally contend over its authenticity; whether original or an early gloss, all agree on what it teaches. David's righteousness was real but not perfect, and the honest naming of his worst sin alongside his highest commendation is itself instructive: the Scripture that holds him up as the pattern for kings will not flatter him, and the same grace that spared his repentant house is the grace that now keeps a lamp burning for his unworthy descendants. The single exception thus does double duty in the narrative: it keeps the praise of David honest, and it warns the reader that the pattern-king was himself a pardoned sinner, so that no descendant may presume upon the promise as though it rested on human desert rather than on divine mercy.
6:
This verse — there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all the days of his life — puzzles the witnesses, because Rehoboam is already dead, and they divide over whether the name is right. John Gill defends the received reading by referring 'his life' to Abijam: the war 'all the days of his life' means all the days of Abijam, in which even as a young man he was engaged, Rehoboam being named here as the head of the house much as Rehoboam himself is elsewhere called David.

Adam Clarke makes the textual case openly. First he limits the 'war': it 'can mean no more than a continual spirit of hostility... frequent skirmishes between bordering parties,' never open battle, since pitched war was forbidden (1 Kings 12:24) and had been noted already (1 Kings 14:30). Then he asks why the notice is repeated and the history of Abijam interrupted, and answers that 'Rehoboam' is probably not the true reading but should be 'Abijam' — the reading of fourteen of Kennicott's and De Rossi's manuscripts, of the Syriac ('Abia the son of Rehoboam') and Arabic, of some copies of the Targum, and of the first printed Vulgate. This, he concludes, 'is doubtless the true reading,' since the memorable war was between Abia and Jeroboam (2 Chron. 13:3).

Charles Ellicott agrees that something is amiss without endorsing the emendation: the verse is omitted in the Vatican Septuagint, and 'the repetition of the notice of Rehoboam, in spite of some artificial explanations, seems inexplicable — probably there is error in the text.' Keil and Delitzsch, on the other hand, save the received wording as Gill does, taking 'all the days of his life' to mean so long as Abijam lived and reigned, which harmonizes with the very next verse's notice of war between Abijam and Jeroboam (v.7). The debate is real but bounded: whether to read 'Abijam' for 'Rehoboam' (Clarke, with Ellicott suspecting error), or to keep 'Rehoboam' and refer 'his life' to Abijam (Gill, Keil). All agree on the fact behind the words — a long, low-grade hostility between the two kingdoms that weakened both.
7:
The closing formula of Abijam's reign is spare, and only two witnesses linger over it, but they draw out its two halves. On the rest of the acts of Abijam... are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?, John Gill notes the likely authorship of those royal annals: they 'seem to be written by Iddo the prophet' (compare 2 Chron. 13:22), the canonical record preserving only what the Spirit selected from a fuller court history that has perished.

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the chronology folded into the same paragraph. Since Asa ascended the throne in Jeroboam's twentieth year, and his father Abijam had begun to reign in Jeroboam's eighteenth, Abijam 'can only have reigned two years and a few months, and not three full years' — which is precisely the rounding Gill had already urged at v.2. The 'three years' of the record is a regnal reckoning that counts the fragments of the first and last years as whole ones, a convention the reader must learn if the numbers of Kings are to be squared with one another.

The verse also quietly reopens the question of the last: its second clause, there was war between Abijam and Jeroboam, names Abijam where the previous verse had named Rehoboam, and so lends weight to those who suspected an error or a shorthand there. Between them the two commentators leave a modest but real point. The sacred historian is not writing an exhaustive biography but a theological one; he passes over Abijam's wars and works — including the great victory the Chronicler records — with a bare reference to the annals, because his interest is not in the king's might but in his heart and in the covenant that outlives him. What God thought worth preserving of a three-year reign is chiefly the verdict already given: that Abijam was spared for David's sake and buried among his fathers, the throne passing at once to a son who would do better. The brevity of the notice is itself a kind of judgment on the brevity and barrenness of the reign.
8:
The verse is the barest of epitaphs — Abijam slept with his fathers; and they buried him in the city of David: and Asa his son reigned in his stead — and only John Gill pauses over it, glossing 'slept with his fathers' with the plain sense, 'that is, died as they did.' The figure is the settled biblical idiom for the death of the faithful and unfaithful alike, and its very gentleness is worth noticing: even this king whose heart was not perfect is granted the language of rest and of gathering to his people.

The witnesses read the three movements of the verse as a single, quiet turning of the page. First, the death: a reign of scarcely three years is closed without lament, the proud victor over Jeroboam laid down among the very ancestors whose promise, not his own merit, had upheld him. Second, the burial in the city of David: he is honoured with a place among the kings, the dignity of the house of David covering a son who did little to deserve it — the covenant faithfulness that gave him a lamp in life follows him into the grave. Third, and most important to the narrator, the succession: Asa his son reigned in his stead.

That last clause is the hinge of the whole chapter, and Matthew Henry had already caught its point at the outset — that God cut Abijam off early 'to make way for his son Asa, who would be a better man.' The reader is meant to feel the relief of it. The decline that ran from Solomon through Rehoboam to Abijam is not allowed to run on; the lamp is not only kept burning but at last passes into worthier hands. Where the northern throne, as the coming verses will show, lurches from one bloody usurper to the next, the southern throne descends peacefully from father to son within the one covenanted line. The brevity of the obituary is itself eloquent: a mixed and forgettable reign gives way, by God's timing, to a long and reforming one, and the true continuity of Judah is not the merit of its kings but the steadiness of the promise made to David.
9:
With Asa the narrative turns from a brief bad reign to a long good one, and the witnesses set the stage. John Gill handles the synchronism — Asa began to reign in Jeroboam's twentieth year — and refers the tangled chronology to his fuller note. Matthew Henry opens the reign with a reflection he draws from the whole run of Judah's kings: their number, good and bad, is nearly equal, 'but the reign of the good kings was generally long, and that of the bad kings short,' so that the state of God's church is not so bad within the period as it first appears. Length of days, he reminds us, is in Wisdom's right hand, and the promise attached to honouring one's father — 'much more thy heavenly Father, that thy days may be long' — is written large in Asa's forty-one years.

Henry sketches in advance the shape the following verses will fill out. Asa 'did that which was right in the eyes of the LORD, and that is right indeed which is so in God's eyes'; he was like David, though neither prophet nor psalmist as David was — and Henry turns even that into comfort: 'if we come up to the graces of those that have gone before us it will be our praise with God, though we come short of their gifts.' The secret of the reign is a single word from the record: his heart was perfect with the LORD, meaning not that he was sinless but that 'he was both cordial and constant in his religion,' sincere and steady, acting from a good principle with a single eye to God's glory.

His times, Henry says, were 'times of reformation,' and reformation has two motions — removing what is evil and re-establishing what is good — both of which the chapter will detail. The two witnesses do not differ; together they mark the change of key. After three reigns of decline the throne of David comes to a man who will make David his pattern, and the historian pauses on the length of the reign as itself a token of divine favour. The reader is invited to watch how a sincere heart works itself out in public life — in the courage to purge idolatry even from the palace, and in the frailty that will still, near the end, lean on carnal help rather than on the God who had so long prospered him.
10:
The record of Asa's forty-one years is joined to a puzzle: his mother's name was Maachah, the daughter of Abishalom — the very words used of Abijam's mother at v.2. John Gill only remarks on the length of the reign, which reached and even exceeded the years of David and Solomon, a blessing on a good king. The others tackle the difficulty head-on.

Keil and Delitzsch refuse the crude solution that Abijam had his own mother for a wife — a reading Thenius entertained, and then built textual conjectures upon. Following Ephraem Syrus, the Rabbins, and others, they explain that Maachah, Abijam's mother, 'continued during Asa's reign to retain the post of queen-mother,' the gebirah or sultana valide, until Asa deposed her for idolatry (v.13) — probably because Asa's own mother had died young. Charles Ellicott reaches the same conclusion: Maachah was the wife of Rehoboam and therefore Asa's grandmother, who 'still retained the place of queen-mother, to the exclusion of the real mother of the king.'

Adam Clarke approaches by way of the loose Hebrew idiom of kinship. Our translators, he notes, thought 'grandmother' the likely meaning and set it in the margin; and citing Calmet he observes that Scripture 'gives the name of daughter indifferently to the niece, the granddaughter, and great-granddaughter,' so that 'daughter of Abishalom' need not mean an immediate child of Absalom at all. The witnesses are here wholly agreed, and the agreement is instructive. What looks at first like a contradiction — the same woman named as mother of two successive kings — dissolves once the office of gebirah is understood. The queen-mother held a recognized station in the court, and Maachah kept it into her grandson's reign. That recognition sets up the drama of the coming verses, where Asa's zeal for God will require him to strip his own grandmother of her dignity for the sake of a pure worship — reformation reaching, as Henry will say, even into the royal household. The apparent contradiction, then, is no contradiction at all but a window onto the institutions of the court, and the witnesses treat it not as an error in the record but as an invitation to read the two verses together and see how long the sway of one idolatrous woman had persisted at the very centre of the kingdom.
11:
The summary verdict on Asa — he did that which was right in the eyes of the LORD, as did David his father — is the standard by which the whole reign is measured, and the witnesses unfold it. John Gill takes 'as did David his father' broadly: 'in his personal walk and conversation, in his government of the nation, and especially in the matters of his God, and of religion, he made David his pattern and example to copy after.' The comparison is not to David's gifts but to David's fidelity.

Keil and Delitzsch immediately translate the general praise into the concrete acts the next verses list: as ruler Asa 'walked in the ways of his pious ancestor David' — banishing the male prostitutes, abolishing the idolatrous abominations his fathers Abijam and Rehoboam had introduced, and deposing his grandmother Maacah for making an idol. The word rendered 'idols,' they add, is gillulim, a term of contempt for idols (Lev. 26:30) meaning not 'dung-gods,' as the Rabbins fancied, but 'logs' or 'masses of stone.'

Charles Ellicott lifts the verse to its place in the larger story. This reign was 'a turning-point in the history of Judah': freed from immediate pressure by Abijah's victory, Asa resolved — perhaps under the guidance of the prophets Azariah and Hanani (2 Chron. 15:1; 16:7) — to renew his kingdom's true strength by restoring the worship of the true God, extirpating by repeated efforts the false worships of Rehoboam and Abijah, and solemnly renewing the covenant in the name of the people and of the strangers who joined them from Ephraim, Manasseh, and Simeon. Of all this (2 Chron. 14–15) the text here gives only brief notice; the same record adds that Asa fortified his cities, strengthened his army, and repelled with great slaughter the vast invasion of Zerah the Ethiopian in his fifteenth year. The witnesses agree without dispute: the whole worth of Asa's kingship is that, unlike his father, he took the sincere heart of David for his rule, and let that heart show itself in a costly, thoroughgoing reformation.
12:
The first motion of reformation is the removal of evil, and the verse names two targets: the cult-prostitutes and the idols. On he took away the sodomites out of the land, John Gill understands a firm execution of the law — Asa cleared out those who had multiplied under Rehoboam and continued under Abijam, 'either by driving them out of the land, or by putting them to death according to the law of God,' as many as came to his knowledge, though the record shows some later remained. Adam Clarke works the Hebrew: the word is haqqedeshim, 'literally the holy or consecrated ones,' a bitter irony in a title given to those consecrated to the licentious rites of the high places (see 1 Kings 14:24).

On the second target, he removed all the idols that his fathers had made, Keil and Delitzsch gather the whole reforming sequence of the next verses into one view: Asa put away the sodomites and all the idols, deposed Maacah for her idol, burned it by the Kidron, brought the dedicated things into the house of the LORD, and — the one shortfall — left the high places standing. The verb 'made' is pointed: the idols Asa destroyed were the very works of his own father and grandfather, so that his reformation was also a repudiation of his immediate house.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as the courage of a sincere heart turned against inherited sin. Where Abijam had 'walked in all the sins of his father,' Asa deliberately undid them, refusing to let idolatry pass as an heirloom. The two evils named are telling: the moral corruption of sanctioned vice and the religious corruption of false worship stand together, for the high places bred both. Matthew Henry's summary catches the order the text itself follows — 'there reformation begins,' with the removal of evil, before the re-establishing of good — and marvels that so much work of this kind was found to do only twenty years after Solomon's death, so quickly and deeply had corruption spread and taken root. Asa's greatness is that he did not leave it to spread further, but struck at it while his hand was in.
13:
Reformation now reaches the palace, and the witnesses are struck that Asa spared not even his own grandmother. On he removed her from being queen, John Gill offers a range of senses — he stripped Maachah of her share in the government she may have held during his minority, or forbade her the court, or, most likely, removed her 'from presiding over the rites of the idol next mentioned.' The point is that her rank as queen-mother did not shield her when her rank had become a patronage of idolatry.

The nature of the idol draws the closest attention. Adam Clarke canvasses the versions on the rare word miphletseth — Vulgate, Septuagint, Syriac, Arabic, Chaldee, and Rabbi Solomon Jarchi — and concludes 'it is pretty evident that the image was a mere Priapus, or something of the same nature,' worshipped by Maachah with impure rites in her grove; 'Maachah was most likely another Messalina, and Asa probably did for his mother what Claudius did for his wife.' Charles Ellicott agrees on the substance while correcting the rendering: the word signifies a 'horrible abomination' of some monstrous kind, and instead of 'in a grove' we should read 'for an asherah,' the wooden emblem of the Canaanite deity (1 Kings 14:23); some obscene emblem is meant, substituted as a still greater abomination for the ordinary asherah, and matched in the parallel 2 Chron. 15:16.

Both note the further steps. Asa 'destroyed her idol, and burnt it by the brook Kidron,' a public defacing that Henry likens to Moses grinding the golden calf to powder (Exod. 32:20) and strewing the ashes in contempt. And he degraded her, in her old age, from her high dignity — a step 'on which the historian here lays great stress,' says Ellicott, precisely because it was so unusual. The lesson the witnesses draw, with Henry, is that 'reformation must begin at home,' and that bad practices will never be suppressed in the country while they are supported at court. Asa honoured his grandmother in everything else; but he loved God better, and would by no means connive at idolatry even for a relation he was bound to reverence.
14:
The reformer's record carries a shadow: the high places were not removed, and yet Asa's heart was perfect with the LORD all his days. The witnesses work to hold the two clauses together. John Gill distinguishes the high places: those meant here were used 'for the worship of God, before the temple was built,' which now should have gone since sacrifice belonged to the temple alone; Asa may have thought them still lawful, or found the people so attached that removal was difficult and dangerous — whereas the high places of open idolatry he certainly did remove.

Adam Clarke presses the same distinction and softens the fault: Asa 'was not able to make a thorough reformation,' which was reserved for his son Jehoshaphat; and the high places he left were probably those where the true God alone was worshipped, Jarchi holding that these were places individuals had erected for Jehovah. Charles Ellicott is less lenient. Comparing the Chronicles, he finds an attempt at this very reform that was not carried through, the craving for local and visible sanctuaries proving 'too strong for even earnest reformers'; the historian, writing by later experience, dwells on this imperfection again and again.

The real difficulty is how the record can still call such a heart perfect, and here Matthew Henry gives the classic answer. The failure yields 'a comfortable note, that those may be found honest and upright with God, and be accepted of him, who yet, in some instances, come short of doing the good they might and should do.' The perfection made the condition of the new covenant 'is not to be understood of sinlessness — then were we all undone — but sincerity.' The witnesses thus divide gently over the high places: whether they were tolerable because devoted to the true God (Gill, Clarke) or a genuine blemish the historian laments (Ellicott). But they close ranks on the deeper matter. A perfect heart is a sincere and constant one, not a flawless one; Asa's real and costly zeal against idolatry was accepted of God even where his reformation stopped short, and the un-removed high places stand as a standing warning that even the best reforms leave something for the next generation to finish.
15:
The second motion of reformation — re-establishing what is good — appears in the bringing of dedicated treasures into the temple, and the witnesses trace their origin. John Gill takes 'the things which his father had dedicated' to be the spoils Abijam had devoted to religious uses in his war with Jeroboam but had not lived to present; so Asa performed his father's vow for him, evidence that Abijam, for all his sins, retained some regard for God's worship. Adam Clarke adds only that Asa 'thought himself bound by the vow of his father,' honouring an obligation he had not himself incurred.

Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch supply the history behind the gold and silver and vessels. They were, says Ellicott, 'the spoils of his own victory over the Egyptian army and Abijah's victory over Jeroboam,' which 'replenished for a time the treasury, swept bare in the reign of Rehoboam by the host of Shishak.' Keil concurs, tracing the offerings to the booty Abijam gathered (2 Chron. 13:16–17) and to Asa's own conquest of the Cushites (2 Chron. 14:12–13). What plunderers had emptied, victory given by God now refilled, and Asa turned the increase not to his own treasury but to the house of the LORD.

The witnesses read the act as the positive counterpart of the purge. Matthew Henry draws the moral plainly: 'we must not only cease to do evil, but learn to do well; not only cast away the idols of our iniquity, but dedicate ourselves and our all to God's honour and glory.' Reformation that only demolishes is half a work; Asa both cleared the court of its abominations and enriched the temple with consecrated spoil. There is a quiet piety, too, in the son completing the father's unfulfilled vow — the good that Abijam intended but neglected is at last performed by the better man who succeeded him, so that even a mixed reign leaves something for a faithful heir to redeem. The dedicated things brought in are the visible token that Asa's zeal was not merely negative but grateful, rendering to God the things that are his.
16:
The chapter turns to Asa's long conflict with Baasha of Israel, and the witnesses begin by fixing what 'war all their days' can and cannot mean. John Gill reckons the phrase 'as long as they lived together,' since Baasha died years before Asa; he adds Abarbanel's shrewd observation that Baasha is introduced here out of order — before the account of his usurpation — because the historian, having given Asa's good deeds, chose to relate his failings before proceeding with the rest of his story.

Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott fill in the decade of peace the Chronicles record. After Abijah's victory Judah had rest ten years (2 Chron. 14:1); Asa spent it exterminating idolatry, fortifying cities, and equipping his army, until the Cushite Zerah invaded and was totally defeated by the help of the Lord, after which Asa, encouraged by the prophet Azariah, renewed his reforms and held a great festival of covenant in his fifteenth year (2 Chron. 15). Only then, says Ellicott, did open war with Israel break out; the standing formula means the old hostile relations, perhaps with some border fighting, not continuous pitched battle.

Adam Clarke raises the chronological knot that has vexed harmonists. 'There was no open war till the thirty-sixth year of Asa,' when Baasha built Ramah (2 Chron. 16:1); but this cannot agree with the record that Baasha's son Elah was killed in Asa's twenty-sixth year, for Baasha 'could not make war upon Asa in the thirty-sixth year of his reign when he was dead in the twenty-sixth.' Clarke offers the harmonists' solution — that the years are counted not from Asa's reign but from the division of the kingdoms — while frankly allowing that otherwise 'there is a mistake in some of the numbers,' most likely in the parallel places. The witnesses thus agree on the substance and debate only the arithmetic: the two kingdoms were structurally at enmity from the schism, and the fraternal division that set brother against brother weakened both and made each an easier prey to their common enemies. Gill's note that Baasha is introduced 'out of course' belongs to the same reading: the historian arranges his material by theme rather than by strict sequence, closing the good account of Asa's reforms before he turns to the failings and the long northern war, so that the whole of the good king is seen together before the shadows fall.
17:
Baasha's aggression — he went up against Judah, and built Ramah — is read by the witnesses for its chronology, its geography, and its strategy. On the date, John Gill insists that the 'thirty-sixth year' of the Chronicles is not of Asa's reign but 'of his kingdom' — that is, of the kingdom of Judah reckoned from the division: seventeen years under Rehoboam, three under Abijam, and the sixteenth of Asa make thirty-six — which is how the Jewish chronology and the best chronologers compute it, since Baasha, dead in Asa's twenty-seventh year, could not have warred in his thirty-sixth.

On the place, Adam Clarke notes that Ramah 'signifies a high place,' a hill commanding the chief road to Jerusalem, about two leagues north of the city, which Baasha fortified 'to prevent all intercourse with the kingdom of Judah, lest his subjects should cleave to the house of David.' Charles Ellicott identifies it with the modern Er-Ram, a Benjamite town some five miles north (Joshua 18:25), and finds a vivid parallel: 'like the fortification of Decelea near Athens in the Peloponnesian war,' this fortress close to the hostile capital was a standing menace. Its purpose, he adds, was partly to stop the defections the Chronicles report (2 Chron. 15:9–10), as many fell away from Israel to Asa in the height of his prosperity.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the account forward to Asa's response: to avert the danger, he sought to induce the Syrian Benhadad to break his league with Baasha and to become his ally. The witnesses do not divide; they read the episode as the first move in a crisis that will expose the one great flaw in a godly king. Baasha's strategy was shrewd and threatening — an enemy fortifying a frontier town so near Jerusalem could not be suffered — but the manner of Asa's deliverance, not the reality of the danger, is what the narrator means to weigh. The blockade of Ramah sets the trap into which Asa's faith, so lately proved against Zerah, will stumble. The precision with which the commentators locate Ramah and reckon the year is not idle antiquarianism; it anchors the crisis in real ground and real time, so that the spiritual failure of the next verses is felt as the choice of an actual king in an actual strait, not a moral abstraction.
18:
Asa's countermeasure — emptying the treasuries of temple and palace to hire Benhadad — is where the witnesses locate the king's great fall, though several also feel the pressure he was under. John Gill notes that the silver and gold were 'what was left untaken away by Shishak' (1 Kings 14:26) or what Abijam and Asa had themselves dedicated, and lays the finger on the sin: such treasures 'were not to be taken, especially the treasures of the house of the LORD, and put to profane use, and particularly to such bad purposes as these were.' Adam Clarke concurs that Shishak had not taken all, or fresh treasure had since come in, and identifies Benhadad as the grandson of Rezon (here called Hezion), founder of the Damascus kingdom (1 Kings 11:23–24).

Charles Ellicott reads the move in its wider politics. Syria, recovering independence at the fall of Solomon's empire, 'was already attaining the formidable power which so soon threatened to destroy Israel'; Baasha had tried to supersede the old Syro-Judean league by a closer alliance, and Asa 'naturally resolved to bribe Benhadad to prefer the old tie to the new.' But, Ellicott insists, 'he went beyond this,' proposing a combined attack and 'for the first time calling in a heathen power against his brethren, the children of Israel' — an expedient which, though it succeeded for the moment, was both a desperate policy and an unfaithfulness to the covenant brotherhood, and so 'deserved and received prophetic rebuke' (2 Chron. 16:7–9), just as Isaiah would later denounce trust in confederacies and alliance with Egypt (Isa. 30:1–17).

Matthew Henry states the spiritual root the others circle. Asa's league with Benhadad 'arose from unbelief': he could not trust the God who had done so much for him to free him from this strait without carnal means, and so 'unbelief makes way for carnal policy, and thus for one sin after another.' The witnesses agree on the double offence — tempting a king to break faith, and robbing God's house to do it — and on its lesson: that even a true believer, so lately victorious by faith against a vast army, may in urgent danger fail to trust the Lord with all his heart, and that some who once shone brightly are covered with a cloud toward the end of their days.
19:
Asa's message to Benhadad — there is a league between me and thee... come and break thy league with Baasha — is read by the witnesses as a study in tempting another to sin. John Gill untangles the diplomacy: though Hezion, if he be the Rezon who troubled Solomon, had been an adversary to Israel, his son proved a confederate with the kings of both Israel and Judah, so that a standing treaty already existed which Asa now sought to turn to his own account.

Adam Clarke is blunt about the whole transaction. Reading the words as a proposal — 'Let there be a league between me and thee, as there was between my father and thy father' — he judges the enterprise needless and sordid: 'there was no reason why Asa should have emptied his treasures at this time to procure the aid of the Syrian king, as it does not appear that there was any danger which himself could not have turned aside.' Rather, Clarke suspects, Asa 'wished to destroy the kingdom of Israel, and to effect this purpose even robbed the house of the LORD.' Keil and Delitzsch carry the request to its result: Benhadad consented and sent his captains against the northern cities of Israel, compelling Baasha to abandon Ramah.

The witnesses do not differ over the morality of the appeal. To ask Benhadad to 'break thy league with Baasha' was to invite him to violate the public faith, and, as Matthew Henry observed, if it was wrong for Benhadad to do it, Asa did wrong in persuading him to it. The verse exposes the logic of unbelief in motion: having declined to rest in the God who had lately saved him, Asa must now buy a pagan king, and buy him by teaching him to be faithless. There is a bitter aptness in the plea 'as between my father and thy father,' for it appeals to a merely human bond of treaty at the very moment the king is neglecting the covenant bond with the LORD. The immediate policy would work; but the witnesses, with the Chronicler's prophet, see in it a victory bought at the cost of faith, and the seed of the wars and troubles that would darken Asa's later years.
20:
Benhadad's swift campaign — smiting Ijon, and Dan, and Abel-beth-maachah, and all Cinneroth, with all the land of Naphtali — the witnesses treat mainly as geography, and through geography as strategy. John Gill notes only that Benhadad broke off his alliance with Israel and, having a standing army with its officers, sent them directly to seize Israel's cities. Adam Clarke observes that he 'appears to have attacked and taken those towns which constituted the principal strength of the kingdom of Israel' — the blow was aimed not at random but at the north's fortified backbone.

Charles Ellicott maps the ground precisely. The region smitten was the mountain country near the source of the Jordan, most exposed to the great northern approach to Israel 'by the entering in of Hamath,' through the wide valley the Greeks called Cœle-Syria — the same tract that would fall again in the later Assyrian invasion (2 Kings 15:29). Ijon he identifies with the modern Tel-Dibbin; Abel-beth-maachah, 'the meadow of the house of Maachah' (2 Sam. 20:14–15), or Abel-maim, lay in the marshy ground north of the waters of Merom; and Cinneroth is the name afterward corrupted into Gennesareth, a region by the lake.

The witnesses draw no debate from the verse, but its place in the narrative is plain enough. The stroke succeeds exactly as Asa had schemed: Benhadad's invasion of the far north forces Baasha to break off his works at Ramah in the south and rush to defend his threatened frontier. What the commentators quietly register, by dwelling on the specific towns of Naphtali laid waste, is the cost of Asa's policy to the wider people of God. Israel is real Israel, the northern tribes of the covenant nation, and their cities are ravaged by a heathen sword hired with the gold of the temple. The immediate deliverance of Judah is purchased by the devastation of Judah's brethren — a success the historian records without applause, and which the prophet will presently rebuke as the fruit of leaning on Syria rather than on the LORD. The very fullness of Ellicott's geography — the named towns, the valley of Cœle-Syria, the exposed line of the northern frontier — makes the reader feel the reach of the ruin, a whole province of the covenant people burned over so that one southern king might escape a siege.
21:
Baasha's withdrawal — when Baasha heard thereof, he left off building of Ramah, and dwelt in Tirzah — the witnesses read as the plan working exactly as intended. John Gill supplies the sense of 'heard thereof': Baasha learned 'what was doing in the northern part of his kingdom,' and had to abandon his southern project to meet the greater threat. Keil and Delitzsch render the verb exactly — he 'remained at Tirzah, his place of residence' — and Adam Clarke notes that Tirzah 'seems to have been the royal city' (v.33; 1 Kings 14:17), in which Baasha was now 'probably obliged to shut himself up.'

Charles Ellicott reads the retreat in two motions: 'in the first instance retiring to meet the new enemy in the north, and then obliged to give up his attempt against Asa.' He also glimpses its longer consequence. From a later notice (1 Kings 20:34) it appears that, until the time of Ahab, Syria retained its conquests and a certain supremacy over Israel; Baasha 'may have had to buy peace by undertaking to leave Judah unmolested,' Judah being now virtually a tributary or client of Syria.

The witnesses find no controversy in the verse, but its irony is not lost on them. The frontier fortress meant to shut Judah in is itself abandoned, and the aggressor is driven back to hole up in his capital. Asa's stratagem has succeeded on every visible count: the blockade is broken, the enemy is in retreat, and the materials of Ramah lie ready to be carried off. Yet the reader who remembers the Chronicler's prophet knows that this glittering success has planted a longer trouble — a Syria strengthened and emboldened on Israel's border, and a Judah that has learned to lean on Damascus. The verse marks the high point of the carnal policy and the beginning of its hidden cost: what unbelief buys cheaply today it often pays for dearly tomorrow. Ellicott's glance ahead to the days of Ahab, when Syria still lorded it over Israel, lets the witnesses read Baasha's retreat as more than a local reverse: the gold of the temple has purchased not lasting security but a stronger and nearer rival, and the deliverance so cleverly contrived has quietly reshaped the balance of power against the people of God.
22:
Asa's exploitation of his advantage — a levy of all Judah to strip Ramah and refortify his own border — the witnesses admire for its energy and read for its topography. John Gill notes that the proclamation 'summoned men of all sorts, ranks, and degrees,' and Adam Clarke presses the phrase none was exempted: this was a general levée en masse, every man obliged to help dismantle the fortress, for 'where the boundaries are to be fortified against an enemy, all exemptions cease' — a maxim of civil policy invoked when the state is in danger.

Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew 'no one being free' and locate the two cities Asa built with the salvaged stone and timber: Geba, 'not to be confounded with Gibeah of Benjamin,' the present Jeba northeast of Ramah (Joshua 18:24), and Mizpah, the present Nebi Samwil to its southwest (Joshua 18:26). Charles Ellicott adds that Asa 'was not content to destroy or occupy the hostile fortress, but pushed his own fortifications further on': Geba, a priestly city and the scene of Jonathan's victory over the Philistines (1 Sam. 13:3), remained Judah's northern outpost even in later days (2 Kings 23:8); and Mizpah stood at the ridge afterward called Scopus, 'the watch-tower,' from which the traveller first sees Jerusalem.

The witnesses draw no dispute, but they register the neat justice of the episode. The very stones with which Baasha had meant to imprison Judah are carried off to fortify Judah's own frontier; the aggressor's materials become the defender's bulwark. Asa's political skill is real, and the narrator does not deny it. Yet the reader is left to weigh this bright administrative success against the means by which the opportunity was bought — for the same king who could so wisely turn an enemy's ruin to his own strength had been unable, a few verses back, simply to trust the God who had already given him a greater victory without Syrian gold. The building of Geba and Mizpah crowns the stratagem; the prophet's rebuke, which the Chronicler preserves, will keep it from being a pattern to imitate.
23:
The closing formula of Asa's reign carries a sting in its tail — nevertheless in the time of his old age he was diseased in his feet — and the witnesses weigh both halves. John Gill observes that some of 'the rest of the acts of Asa' are found in the canonical Chronicles and others in the fuller royal annals from which Scripture drew. Charles Ellicott notes that the phrase all his might, used of neither Rehoboam nor Abijah, is significant, marking 'the increased power of Judah under Asa,' whose fortification of cities (2 Chron. 14:6) was the traditional policy of a small kingdom hemmed in by stronger neighbours.

The disease draws the gravest comment. Adam Clarke takes it for 'a strong rheumatic affection, or the gout,' beginning in the thirty-ninth year of the reign, three years before death, and adds the pointed detail from the Chronicles: 'he sought to physicians rather than to the Lord' (2 Chron. 16:12). Keil and Delitzsch read the same fact as a spiritual decline: that Asa 'sought help from the physicians, but not from the Lord,' shows 'that the longer he lived the more he turned his heart away from the Lord.' Ellicott sharpens it further — in his last days Asa 'ventured to defy the prophetic authority by the imprisonment of Hanani the seer,' so that prosperity 'had somewhat deteriorated his character,' though he remained faithful to the worship of God.

The witnesses do not quite debate, but they range along a spectrum: Gill and Clarke chiefly report the disease, while Keil and Ellicott read it, with the Chronicler, as a chastening that exposed a heart increasingly self-reliant. Together they draw the chapter's most sober lesson about a good man's later years. The very king who as a young reformer had trusted God against a countless army came, in age and pain, to trust physicians and Syrian gold and his own power, and to silence the prophet who rebuked him. Asa's feet, that could not carry him, become an emblem: the walk that had once been upright with the LORD faltered at the last, a warning that early grace must be renewed daily lest prosperity harden into unbelief.
24:
Asa's death and honourable burial close the account of Judah's reign, and the witnesses mark chiefly the dignity of his passing. John Gill pictures the funeral, gathering the detail the Chronicler adds: Asa 'was buried with his fathers in the city of David his father, in a sepulchre there he himself had made,' laid 'on a bed filled with sweet odours and spices, prepared according to art,' which were burned for him in a great and solemn ceremony (2 Chron. 16:14). Adam Clarke likewise points to that record of 'his splendid and costly funeral,' a public honour befitting a long and, on the whole, faithful reign.

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the succession and the turn of the narrative: Jehoshaphat his son reigned in his stead, and with the next words the historian crosses back to the northern kingdom to take up the reign of Nadab. The quiet clause of succession is itself a testimony. For all Asa's late faults, the throne of David passes once more from father to son within the covenanted line, and passes to a son who — as the coming books will show — would carry the reformation further than his father had.

The witnesses raise no dispute, but the placement of the verse invites the contrast the whole chapter is built upon. Asa 'slept with his fathers' and was laid among the kings with spices and mourning, his son already secure on the throne; and immediately the text will turn to Israel, where kings do not sleep with their fathers but are cut down by conspirators, and whole houses are butchered to clear the way for usurpers. The peaceful, honoured death of a Davidic king, succeeded by his own son, is set deliberately against the bloody instability across the border. Even a reign that ended under a cloud of disease and prophetic rebuke closes in the rest and continuity that belong to the house God had promised to establish — a rest the northern dynasties, resting on violence rather than on covenant, could never know. Gill's and Clarke's dwelling on the pomp of the funeral is not idle; the spices burned and the costly rites are the public honour a nation pays a king it owns, and Judah owned Asa to the end, laying him among his fathers as one of David's true sons. That honour, granted even after his late failures, is itself a testimony to the stability the covenant conferred on the whole line, a dignity the murdered and unmourned kings of the north were never accorded.
25:
The scene shifts to the northern kingdom, and the witnesses set Nadab's brief reign in its grim frame. John Gill only fixes the synchronism — Nadab began to reign in Asa's second year, before Baasha — but Matthew Henry opens the whole tragic panorama. While Judah was happy under Asa's good government, Israel was 'as a reed shaken in the water' (ch. 14:15): during Asa's single reign 'the government of their kingdom was in six or seven different hands' — Jeroboam, Nadab, Baasha, Elah, Zimri, Tibni, and Omri, 'undermining and destroying one another.' 'This they got,' Henry says, 'by deserting the house both of God and of David.'

Of Nadab himself Henry writes with a preacher's regret. Had the death of his brother Abijah (ch. 14:1) moved him to religion, and the honour done at that death engaged him to follow a good example, 'his reign might have been long and glorious.' Instead 'he walked in the way of his father,' kept up the worship of the calves, and forbade his subjects to go to Jerusalem, so that he 'sinned and made Israel to sin,' and God brought ruin on him quickly. Besieging Gibbethon, a city the Philistines had taken from the Danites, he was murdered in the midst of his own army by Baasha; and 'so little interest had he in the affections of his people' that the army did not avenge him but chose his murderer for his successor.

The two witnesses read the reign as the beginning of the northern kingdom's self-destruction, and as the visible working-out of prophecy. What Ahijah had foretold against the house of Jeroboam now begins to fall; the calf-worship that Jeroboam devised to secure his throne becomes the very sin that dooms his line. Henry's contrast is the chapter's spine: Judah, cleaving to the temple and the covenant, enjoys length of days and orderly succession; Israel, having deserted both God's house and David's, spins through a dizzying series of short and violent reigns. The reader is meant to see that apostasy does not merely offend God in the abstract but unstitches a nation's peace, and that the throne built on Jeroboam's expedient idolatry could not stand.
26:
The verdict on Nadab — he did evil in the sight of the LORD, and walked in the way of his father, and in his sin wherewith he made Israel to sin — the witnesses read as the settled formula of the northern kings. John Gill takes the evil to be idolatry, 'than which nothing is a greater evil in his sight.' Charles Ellicott notes that this 'constantly-recurring phrase' signifies, as the context shows, 'perseverance in the idolatrous system introduced by Jeroboam' — not fresh invention but dogged continuance in the calf-worship that had defined the dynasty.

Keil and Delitzsch identify the 'sin' precisely as 'the calf-worship introduced by Jeroboam' (1 Kings 12:28), and carry the account forward: when Nadab in the second year of his reign besieged Gibbethon, which the Philistines had occupied, Baasha the son of Ahijah, of the tribe of Issachar, conspired against him and slew him, and after becoming king exterminated the whole house of Jeroboam, 'without leaving a single soul,' fulfilling the prediction of Ahijah (1 Kings 14:10). Gibbethon itself, allotted to the Danites (Joshua 19:44), had fallen to the Philistines, and the Israelites besieged it in vain under both Nadab and Baasha.

The witnesses draw no debate here, but they underline the causal chain the verse begins. Nadab's evil is not merely personal wickedness but the maintenance of a system — the golden calves at Bethel and Dan by which Jeroboam had sought to wean Israel from Jerusalem — and it is precisely this persevering sin that seals the doom of his house. The narrative's shape teaches its theology: the recurring phrase 'wherewith he made Israel to sin' marks each king who keeps the calves, and each such king, in turn, comes under the judgment first spoken against Jeroboam. Nadab inherits his father's throne and his father's idolatry together, and with them his father's sentence; the two years of his reign are enough to show that the entail of that sin runs on unbroken until the whole line is swept away. Keil's careful notice of the vain siege of Gibbethon, pressed by both Nadab and Baasha and never won, quietly frames the whole reign: the northern kings expend themselves against a Philistine town they cannot take, while the real siege, the LORD's long-declared judgment on the calves, closes silently upon their own house.
27:
The rise of Baasha by murder — Baasha the son of Ahijah, of the house of Issachar, conspired against him; and Baasha smote him at Gibbethon — the witnesses read for its actors and its larger meaning. John Gill can say little of the man beyond his tribe, guessing him 'very probably an officer in Nadab's army,' positioned to strike while the king was distracted by the siege. Adam Clarke and Keil and Delitzsch supply the setting: Gibbethon, a town of Dan (Joshua 19:44) generally held by the Philistines, was under siege by 'Nadab and all Israel' when the blow fell.

Charles Ellicott lifts the assassination to its place in the kingdom's history. Baasha, 'sprung from an obscure tribe, hardly at any time distinguished in the history, and himself of low origin in it' (1 Kings 16:2), 'is the first of the many military chiefs who by violence or assassination seized upon the throne of Israel.' And Ellicott points the contrast that gives the verse its weight: 'the constant succession of ephemeral dynasties stands in striking contrast with the unchanged royalty of the house of David, resting on the promise of God.' The siege itself, he adds, must have been fruitless, for twenty-six years later Gibbethon was still in Philistine hands (1 Kings 16:15).

The witnesses do not dispute, but Ellicott's contrast is the interpretive key they all serve. Judah's throne descends by promise, father to son, through Asa to Jehoshaphat; Israel's throne is seized by the sword, an obscure captain butchering his master over a stalled siege. The very disorder of the northern kingdom — a soldier of nowhere murdering his way to the crown — is the outworking of its founding apostasy, and of the judgment pronounced on Jeroboam's house. What looks like mere political violence is, in the narrator's frame, the hand of God removing a doomed line through the ambition of a wicked man. The reader is being taught to see two kinds of kingship: one anchored in a covenant that no usurper can overturn, and one adrift on force, where each throne is only as secure as the next conspirator's dagger.
28:
The bare notice of the deed — even in the third year of Asa king of Judah did Baasha slay him, and reigned in his stead — draws one plain comment from John Gill, who reads the motive off the outcome: Baasha's slaying of Nadab 'seems to be his only or chief view in slaying him, to get possession of his kingdom.' The murder was not vengeance for wrong nor zeal for God but naked ambition, the throne itself the whole of the prize.

Matthew Henry, surveying the same event, had caught its most damning detail — that Nadab's own army, so far from avenging their murdered king, 'chose his murderer for his successor,' a measure of how little the son of Jeroboam was loved by the people he misled. Henry weighs the possible springs of Baasha's act — 'whether upon a personal pique, or to be avenged on the house of Jeroboam for some affront, or under pretence of freeing his country from a bad prince, or merely from a principle of ambition' — and concludes that it does not appear, but the last is the likeliest; whatever the pretext, Baasha 'slew him and reigned in his stead.'

The witnesses raise no debate, but the verse's terse dating carries a theological weight the narrator means the reader to feel. The bloody exchange of the northern crown is measured, pointedly, by the regnal years of Asa in Judah — in the third year of Asa — as if to hold the two kingdoms up together: while the Davidic king reigns on in Jerusalem in the same long, stable reign, the throne of Israel changes hands by assassination within a single line. Baasha's success is real and immediate; he sits where Nadab sat. Yet the very next verses will show that the man who climbed to the throne over Nadab's corpse becomes, unknowingly, the executioner of a divine sentence, and that the ambition which grasped a crown will one day answer for the blood it shed. The throne seized in the third year of Asa is a throne already under judgment.
29:
Baasha's massacre of the whole house of Jeroboam raises the chapter's sharpest moral question — how a crime can also be a judgment — and the witnesses meet it directly. John Gill reads Baasha's motive plainly: 'he smote all the house of Jeroboam, that he might have no rival, or any that could pretend a title to the crown.' Adam Clarke adds the providential frame: this was 'according to Ahijah's prophetic declaration' (1 Kings 14:10), so that 'God made use of one wicked man to destroy another.'

Charles Ellicott holds both truths together without softening either. He sees 'no reason to suppose that Baasha had any formal mission of vengeance, or that his conspiracy and assassination were due to any motive but his own ambition' — indeed the contrary may be inferred from the later word that judgment came on Baasha himself in part 'because he killed' Nadab and his house (1 Kings 16:7). From this Ellicott draws the principle that governs the whole episode: 'Sin which works out God's purpose is not the less truly sin.' Baasha is at once the free agent of his own ambition and the unwitting instrument of a sentence pronounced a generation before.

The witnesses converge on that double vision, and Matthew Henry states its two sides as one. 'Herein he was barbarous, but God was righteous': the man who left 'not any that breathed' of Jeroboam's line was cruel and self-serving, yet the extirpation fulfilled Ahijah's word (1 Kings 14:14), 'for no word of God shall fall to the ground,' and Jeroboam's sin was thereby punished, since 'those that provoke God do it to their own confusion' (Jer. 7:19). The lesson the commentators press is that divine sovereignty and human guilt are not rivals. God can accomplish his just threatenings through the very ambition and bloodshed he will afterward judge, so that the same act is prophecy fulfilled and crime committed. Baasha's sword clears the throne of a doomed house exactly as God foretold; and Baasha's sword will itself be answered, because the fulfilment of God's purpose never excuses the wickedness of the hand that serves it.
30:
The verse gives the divine reason for the slaughter — because of the sins of Jeroboam which he sinned, and which he made Israel to sin, by his provocation wherewith he provoked the LORD God of Israel to anger — and John Gill catches its precise force. Baasha did not destroy Jeroboam's family out of any hatred of their sins, 'for he walked in the same'; rather 'the Lord threatened this by his prophet, and suffered it to be done because of his abominations.' The clause explains not Baasha's motive but God's permission: the human agent acted from ambition, while the divine purpose behind the event was the punishment of Jeroboam's provoking idolatry.

Matthew Henry had joined the same two thoughts in his survey of the passage: 'Jeroboam's sin was punished, for those that provoke God do it to their own confusion,' and Ahijah's prophecy 'was accomplished, for no word of God shall fall to the ground.' The double naming of the sin in the verse — that Jeroboam both 'sinned' himself and 'made Israel to sin' — marks the peculiar gravity of a ruler's idolatry: he is answerable not only for his own apostasy but for the whole nation he seduced into it, the calves at Bethel and Dan having become a snare to Israel for generations.

The witnesses raise no dispute over so plain a statement, but they will not let its weight be lost. The verse is the theological caption over the whole grim scene of verse twenty-nine: what looked like one usurper's butchery of a rival house is here declared to be the LORD's requital of a specific, named sin. God's judgments are not arbitrary eruptions of power but the measured answer to provocation, and they fall the more surely because they were foretold. There is a solemn justice in the timing, too — Jeroboam had devised the calf-worship to secure his dynasty, and it is that very worship, continued by his son, which now ensures the dynasty's extinction. The sin engineered to preserve the house is the sin that destroys it, and the reader is left with the sober certainty that provoking the God of Israel is a work that recoils, sooner or later, upon the head of the provoker.
31:
The brief closing formula for Nadab — now the rest of the acts of Nadab, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel? — draws only a short gloss from John Gill, who notes that in Nadab's short reign there were acts 'which yet were more than here related,' preserved in the fuller royal annals from which the sacred history selected. The very brevity of the notice is part of its meaning.

The formula itself repays attention, and the witnesses read it against its counterpart in Judah. Where Abijam and Asa were referred to 'the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah,' Nadab is referred to the chronicles of the kings of Israel — two separate registers for two separate kingdoms, the divided monarchy keeping divided records. Matthew Henry's observation over the whole run of these reigns applies here with force: the reigns of the good kings were generally long and the bad kings short, and Nadab's scarcely two years is a case in point. A wicked reign leaves little worth recording in the sacred history beyond the fact of its wickedness and the manner of its end.

The witnesses find no controversy, but the verse quietly makes its theological point. Nadab did much, no doubt, in the way of kings — sieges, policies, the ordinary business of a crown, enough to fill a chronicle — yet the inspired narrator dismisses it all with a single referring question, because none of it touched the one thing that mattered: he walked in his father's sin and made Israel to sin. The 'acts' that men record and the acts that God weighs are not the same. A king may crowd the annals of his kingdom and leave nothing of worth in the book of God, his whole reign summed up in the calves he kept and the throne he lost. The formula that closes Nadab's story is thus a kind of epitaph on a wasted reign — busy, brief, and barren — swept up almost at once in the ruin of his father's house.
32:
The verse repeats, almost word for word, the earlier notice — there was war between Asa and Baasha king of Israel all their days — and the witnesses account for both the substance and the repetition. John Gill explains the enmity from character: Asa, a godly king, 'had no respect' for Baasha, a wicked man and an idolater, however Baasha had destroyed the house of Jeroboam; nor had Baasha 'any regard to Judah, nor to the worship of God at Jerusalem, so that there was no good understanding between them, but frequent acts of hostility.' The division was not incidental but rooted in irreconcilable allegiances — the one kingdom cleaving to the temple, the other to the calves.

On the repetition, Keil and Delitzsch observe simply that this verse 'is a repetition of v.16,' resuming the thread of the Asa-and-Baasha conflict after the narrative had turned aside to recount Nadab's fall and Baasha's bloody accession. Adam Clarke likewise refers the reader back to his note on the earlier verse, treating the two as one standing statement of the continual hostility between the kingdoms.

The witnesses draw no debate, but they let the doubling register its point. The narrator has just told how Baasha rose — by conspiracy, murder, and the massacre of a whole house — and now, resuming the story of the two kings, he reminds us that this same Baasha stood in lifelong enmity with the godly Asa of Judah. The repeated formula binds the two halves of the chapter together and underlines the settled condition of the divided monarchy: a chronic, grinding war between brother-kingdoms, springing ultimately from the schism that tore Israel from the house of David. Gill's explanation is the deepest note. The war 'all their days' was finally a war over worship — a king who loved the LORD's house could have no true peace with a king wedded to Jeroboam's idols — and the enmity between the two thrones is the political shadow cast by a religious apostasy. Keil's plain observation that the verse merely repeats the earlier notice is itself instructive, for the narrator's willingness to say the same thing twice signals how fixed and unchanging that hostility was — not a passing quarrel but the permanent weather of the divided monarchy, resumed here exactly where it had been left.
33:
Baasha's own reign is now formally opened — in the third year of Asa... began Baasha the son of Ahijah to reign over all Israel in Tirzah, twenty and four years — and the witnesses note what the record chooses to stress. John Gill observes that the notice is repeated 'partly to observe that the whole kingdom submitted to him, though an usurper and murderer,' and partly to record his capital and the length of his reign: the phrase over all Israel marks that the man who had waded to the throne through blood was nonetheless accepted by the whole northern nation.

Keil and Delitzsch note that Baasha's reign 'is described very briefly according to its duration and its spirit,' the account moving quickly to the word of the prophet Jehu the son of Hanani against his house (1 Kings 16:1–4), a word that 'coincides exactly, mutatis mutandis, with the words of Ahijah concerning Jeroboam.' They preserve, too, the arresting comment on the perversity of these kings: there was 'something very strange in the perversity and stolidity of the kings of Israel, that when they saw the families of preceding kings evidently overthrown by the command of God on account of the worship of the calves — families they had themselves overturned — they nevertheless worshipped the same calves.'

The witnesses draw no dispute, but that comment is the heart of the verse's meaning. Baasha had been the instrument of Jeroboam's ruin; he had seen, with his own eyes, a whole royal house exterminated for the calf-worship, and had himself carried out the sentence. If any king had reason to break with that idolatry, it was he. Yet he takes the throne 'in Tirzah' and keeps the very calves whose worship had doomed his predecessor — and, as the coming verse will say, receives for it the identical sentence. The repetition of Ahijah's prophecy in Jehu's mouth is not narrative laziness but theological insistence: the same sin will meet the same judgment, again and again, because the kings of Israel are incapable of learning from the graves of the houses they themselves have dug. Twenty-four years of reign will not save a throne built on the sin that destroys thrones.
34:
The chapter closes on Baasha with the same formula it had used of Nadab — he did evil in the sight of the LORD, and walked in the way of Jeroboam, and in his sin wherewith he made Israel to sin — and the witnesses read it as the tragic moral of the whole. John Gill notes the terrible symmetry: Baasha 'did evil in the sight of the Lord, as Nadab did, whom he slew' — the executioner walking in the very path of the man he had executed. Keil and Delitzsch record the same verdict, that Baasha 'walked in the way of Jeroboam, and in his sin wherewith he made Israel to sin,' the identical charge that had sealed the doom of the house he destroyed.

Adam Clarke draws out the deepest lesson of the verse, and of the chapter. 'The entail of iniquity cannot be cut off but by a thorough conversion of the soul to God, and of this these bad kings seem to have had no adequate notion.' Here is why the cycle never breaks: 'the wicked followed the steps of the wicked, and became still more wicked; sin gathers strength by exercise and age.' Baasha had the clearest warning any man could have — he had himself been the sword of God's judgment on Jeroboam's calf-worship — yet he embraced that worship, so that, as Matthew Henry observed, it appeared his destruction of Jeroboam's house sprang 'not from any dislike of Jeroboam's sin but from malice and ambition; for, when he had rooted out the sinner, he himself clave to the sin.'

The witnesses raise no debate; they close ranks on the chapter's governing truth. Two kingdoms have been set side by side. In Judah, a king who made David his pattern reigned long and died in peace, his throne descending to a worthy son within the covenant. In Israel, dynasty devoured dynasty, each new usurper keeping the very sin that had destroyed the last, because none would submit to the thorough conversion that alone could break the entail. Baasha's epitaph is that he learned nothing from the house he butchered. The reformation Asa pursued and the apostasy Baasha perpetuated are the chapter's two roads, and the closing verse leaves the reader in no doubt which one runs down to ruin: sin persisted in, generation after generation, hardens into a fate that only grace can cut.
